text.title: Tree Testing: Six-Step Process and Results Interpretation
text.summary: Six-step process from tree creation through iteration, success-rate thresholds, and path analysis patterns.

rule.statement: Follow this sequence: (1) create text-only tree from IA, (2) write 10-15 scenario tasks covering shallow and deep destinations, (3) define correct and acceptable-alternative destinations per task, (4) run the test with 30-50 participants, (5) analyze per-task metrics and first-click patterns, (6) iterate and retest problem areas.
rule.rationale: Skipping per-task correct-answer definition before launch produces ambiguous success scoring. Defining acceptable alternatives upfront (e.g., multiple valid locations) prevents rater disagreement and inconsistent automated scoring.

rule.statement: Recruit 50 participants minimum for a single-tree test; 30 per arm for an A/B comparison. Below these thresholds, success-rate confidence intervals are too wide for reliable conclusions.
rule.rationale: Success rates from N=5 can vary by 40+ percentage points from the true population rate. N=30 produces confidence intervals narrow enough (~±10pp) to inform IA decisions.

section.title: Success Rate Interpretation
section.p: Thresholds: >80% — excellent, move forward; 60-80% — acceptable, minor fixes; 40-60% — problem area, needs redesign; below 40% — major issue, full redesign required.

rule.statement: Always report per-task metrics with the worst tasks called out explicitly. Never report only the overall site average — aggregating across tasks hides the bottom 20% that require action.
rule.rationale: An overall site success rate of 75% can mask 3 tasks with 30% success rates — each a blocking navigation failure. The average is a reporting artifact; the per-task breakdown is the decision input.

section.title: Path Analysis Patterns

rule.statement: Interpret wrong-path patterns as specific IA fixes: common wrong path = label or structure problem; random paths = label unclear; backtracking = structure does not match mental model; abandonment = content seems not to exist.
rule.rationale: Each wrong-path pattern has a different root cause and a different fix. "Label confusion" → rename the label. "Random paths" → restructure or add cross-links. "Abandonment" → add the item users expected to find.

section.reference: Python script to compute metrics from Treejack CSV export
